content.text: Vocabulary size

is the number of distinct token types a tokenizer can produce -- the alphabet from which every prompt and completion is assembled. GPT-2 uses 50,257; GPT-4's cl100k tokenizer uses 100,277; LLaMA 3 uses 128,000; Gemma 2 pushes to 256,000. Choosing that number is not an aesthetic decision: it governs sequence length, embedding matrix memory, softmax computation time, multilingual coverage, and how gracefully the model handles rare words and code. For anyone building agentic systems where token budgets, latency, and multi-language tool outputs all collide, understanding this trade-off is directly operational knowledge.
What it is
Every subword tokenizer -- BPE, WordPiece, Unigram LM -- is trained to compress a corpus into a fixed set of symbols. Vocabulary size
V

is the cardinality of that set, specified as a hyperparameter before tokenizer training begins. The tokenizer then learns which character sequences deserve their own slot and which should be expressed as sequences of shorter pieces. A word like
tokenization
might occupy one slot in a 128K vocabulary (common enough to merit a dedicated token) but split into three pieces --
token
ization
-- in a 32K vocabulary, or four pieces in an 8K vocabulary.

The vocabulary is fixed at training time. During inference the tokenizer applies its learned merge rules deterministically, producing integer IDs from the vocabulary. Those IDs index into the model's embedding matrix (shape V x d_model) at the input stage and into the output projection (also V x d_model) when the model predicts the next token. Vocabulary size therefore directly governs the size of two of the largest parameter tensors in the model.
Why it matters
The choice propagates into at least five distinct operational concerns that affect model quality, serving cost, and agent design:
Context window utilization.

A 1,000-word document tokenizes to approximately 1,300 tokens at 50K vocab and to approximately 950 tokens at 128K vocab. That 27% difference can be the margin between fitting a document in-context or having to chunk it.
API cost.
Most LLM APIs bill per token. Higher fertility (more tokens per word) inflates cost for the same semantic content.
Embedding matrix memory.

V x d_model x bytes_per_param. At 128K vocab and d_model = 4096 (fp16), the embedding matrix alone is 1 GB -- and in tied-weight models it appears twice, once as input lookup and once as output projection.
Softmax computation.
At each decoding step the model computes logits over all V candidates. Larger V means more FLOPs per generated token and more memory for activation storage during the forward pass.
Multilingual and domain coverage.
A vocabulary trained on English-dominated data will fragment non-Latin scripts heavily, multiplying their token costs by 3-10x relative to English and making cross-lingual transfer harder to learn.
Gradient coverage during training.

Each training step updates only the embedding rows and output weights for tokens that actually appear in the batch. Rare tokens in large vocabularies receive sparse gradient signal and may remain undertrained even after many epochs.
Benefits
Benefits of a larger vocabulary
Lower fertility on the target language.
Common words, morphemes, and code identifiers get single-token representations, shortening sequences and reducing attention cost quadratically with sequence length.
Better rare-word handling.
Technical terms, proper nouns, and domain jargon can occupy dedicated slots rather than fragmenting into sub-morphemic pieces the model must reassemble.
Stronger multilingual coverage.
With enough vocabulary slots, scripts such as Chinese, Arabic, and Devanagari get meaningful subword units instead of falling back to individual characters.
Code tokenization quality.
Identifiers, keywords, and common patterns like
self.
or
def
can be preserved as single tokens, making the model more likely to produce syntactically valid and idiomatically correct code.
Benefits of a smaller vocabulary
Smaller embedding matrix.
Directly reduces parameter count and GPU memory, which matters especially for models being fine-tuned or served on constrained hardware.
Faster softmax.
Fewer logit values to compute and normalize at each decoding step, reducing both FLOPs and memory bandwidth pressure.
Denser gradient updates.
Each token appears more frequently in training, so its embeddings and output weights are updated more often and converge faster from random initialization.
Simpler cross-tokenizer alignment.
When fine-tuning from a checkpoint or adapting a model to a new domain, a smaller vocabulary is easier to extend without catastrophic embedding drift.
Trade-offs
The fundamental tension is that vocabulary size simultaneously controls two competing costs: the
sequence length cost
(decreases as V grows, reducing attention computation and context usage) and the
embedding cost
(increases as V grows, increasing memory and softmax computation). These pull in opposite directions, creating a curve with a practical minimum somewhere in the middle.

The optimal point shifts depending on the target domain. English-only models saturate most of their fertility benefit around 32K-64K tokens. Multilingual models need substantially larger vocabularies -- 100K to 256K -- to give all supported scripts reasonable token efficiency. Code-focused models benefit from vocabularies that include common programming patterns, which may require fewer total slots than a general-purpose multilingual vocabulary.
There is also a
training data requirement

trade-off: a 256K vocabulary has twice as many output slots as a 128K vocabulary. Each slot needs sufficient training exposure to learn a useful representation. Undertrained slots contribute noise at inference time -- the model may avoid those tokens even when they would be the correct prediction, degrading fluency on rare-token inputs.
How it works
Vocabulary construction happens before model training as a separate preprocessing step. The most common algorithm, Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE), works as follows:
Initialize the vocabulary with all individual bytes or Unicode characters (256 bytes provides a lossless baseline that handles any input without unknown tokens).
Scan the training corpus and count every adjacent pair of current symbols.
Merge the most frequent pair into a new single symbol, adding it to the vocabulary.
Repeat steps 2-3 until the vocabulary reaches the target size V.
Record the merge table; this is the tokenizer artifact applied deterministically at inference time.

WordPiece (used in BERT) applies the same iterative logic but selects the merge that maximizes training-data likelihood under a language model assumption rather than raw frequency. Unigram LM (used in some SentencePiece configurations) works in reverse: it starts with a large vocabulary and prunes tokens whose removal causes the smallest increase in corpus encoding cost.

Regardless of algorithm, the output is a vocabulary file mapping tokens to integer IDs and a set of merge rules (BPE) or probability table (Unigram LM). The chosen V determines the ID space the model must handle, the shape of its embedding tensors, and the fertility characteristics of every downstream prompt and completion.
Architecture
The diagram below maps the three vocabulary size regimes to their practical implications and shows the token fertility trade-off across real production tokenizers:

attrs.alt: Three-panel diagram comparing small (8K-32K), sweet-spot (32K-128K), and large (64K-256K) vocabulary sizes. Each panel lists advantages and costs. The center panel shows real model vocabulary sizes: GPT-2 at 50,257; LLaMA 2 at 32,000; GPT-4 at 100,277; LLaMA 3 at 128,000; Gemma 2 at 256,000. A fertility scale at the bottom shows tokens-per-word decreasing from 3.2 at 8K to 1.05 at 256K.

content.text: When to use it
Choose a smaller vocabulary (8K - 32K) when
Training a narrow-domain model on a single language with limited hardware budget and a corpus where most content uses a restricted lexicon.
Building a lightweight embedding or reranking model where parameter count is the primary constraint and context-window efficiency is secondary.
Working with a highly regular domain such as SQL or a single programming language where a small subword set covers nearly all observed patterns.
Running many parallel fine-tuning experiments and needing to minimize GPU memory overhead per experiment to maximize parallelism.
Choose a mid-range vocabulary (32K - 64K) when
Building a general English or English-plus-code model. This range covers the vast majority of English tokens with high efficiency while keeping embedding matrices manageable on single-GPU setups.
Fine-tuning an existing checkpoint where you want to stay close to the base tokenizer and avoid introducing major embedding alignment problems.
Latency is critical and you need predictable tokens-per-second throughput with a fixed softmax computation budget.
Choose a larger vocabulary (64K - 256K) when
Building a multilingual model supporting ten or more languages, especially those with rich morphology or non-Latin scripts where small vocabularies produce extremely high fertility.
Training a code model where preserving common identifiers, operators, and library names as single tokens materially improves generation quality and reduces hallucination of plausible-but-wrong APIs.

Context window cost is the dominant concern and you have enough GPU memory to absorb the larger embedding matrix -- a trade-off that increasingly favors larger vocabularies as models scale to 128K+ context windows.
Following the post-GPT-4 industry trend toward 100K+ vocabularies for frontier general-purpose models designed to handle diverse user inputs reliably.
How to implement
Train tokenizers at several vocabulary sizes on a representative sample of your target corpus, then benchmark fertility before committing to a size. The HuggingFace
tokenizers
library makes this straightforward:

# Train tokenizers at three different vocabulary sizes and compare fertility.
# Requires: pip install tokenizers datasets

from tokenizers import Tokenizer
from tokenizers.models import BPE
from tokenizers.trainers import BpeTrainer
from tokenizers.pre_tokenizers import ByteLevel
from datasets import load_dataset

def train_tokenizer(corpus_lines: list[str], vocab_size: int) -> Tokenizer:
    tokenizer = Tokenizer(BPE(unk_token="<unk>"))
    tokenizer.pre_tokenizer = ByteLevel()
    trainer = BpeTrainer(
        vocab_size=vocab_size,
        special_tokens=["<unk>", "<pad>", "<s>", "</s>"],
        min_frequency=2,
        show_progress=False,
    )
    tokenizer.train_from_iterator(corpus_lines, trainer=trainer)
    return tokenizer

def measure_fertility(tokenizer: Tokenizer, sentences: list[str]) -> float:
    """Tokens-per-word ratio. Lower is better (denser tokenization)."""
    total_tokens = 0
    total_words = 0
    for sentence in sentences:
        encoded = tokenizer.encode(sentence)
        total_tokens += len(encoded.ids)
        total_words += len(sentence.split())
    return total_tokens / total_words if total_words else 0.0

# Load a small English corpus as stand-in
ds = load_dataset("wikitext", "wikitext-2-raw-v1", split="train", trust_remote_code=True)
lines = [row["text"] for row in ds if len(row["text"].strip()) > 40][:5000]

eval_sentences = [
    "The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog.",
    "Transformer architectures have fundamentally reshaped natural language processing.",
    "International tokenization challenges arise with agglutinative morphology.",
    "def calculate_embeddings(input_ids, vocab_size, d_model): pass",
]

for vocab_size in [8_000, 32_000, 50_000, 128_000]:
    tok = train_tokenizer(lines, vocab_size)
    fertility = measure_fertility(tok, eval_sentences)
    actual_size = tok.get_vocab_size()
    print(f"vocab_size={vocab_size:>7,}  actual={actual_size:>7,}  fertility={fertility:.3f}")
A few implementation details are critical. First, always reserve slots for special tokens (
<pad>
<unk>

, and any task-specific tokens) before setting the target vocabulary size -- they come out of your budget of learned subword pieces. Second, ensure your tokenizer training corpus represents the actual distribution of your target task: a tokenizer trained on Wikipedia will fragment Python code more than one trained on a mixed corpus. Third, round your vocabulary size to a multiple of 64 or 128; this aligns embedding matrix dimensions to GPU memory boundaries and can improve throughput by a few percent at no quality cost.
Examples
The script below compares how the same text tokenizes across GPT-2 (50K) and GPT-4 cl100k (100K), showing both token count and characters-per-token as a proxy for compression quality:

# Compare tokenization of the same text across real model tokenizers.
# Requires: pip install tiktoken transformers sentencepiece

import tiktoken
from transformers import AutoTokenizer

TEXT_SAMPLES = [
    # English prose
    "Vocabulary size is one of the most consequential hyperparameters in language model design.",
    # Code
    "def compute_loss(logits, targets, vocab_size): return F.cross_entropy(logits.view(-1, vocab_size), targets.view(-1))",
    # Mixed language / proper nouns
    "The Eiffel Tower (Tour Eiffel) was constructed between 1887 and 1889.",
    # Numbers and symbols
    "Revenue: $1,234,567.89 | Growth: +12.3% | CAGR: 8.7%",
]

tokenizers_to_test = {
    "GPT-2 / cl50k  (50K)":   tiktoken.get_encoding("gpt2"),
    "GPT-4 / cl100k (100K)":  tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base"),
}

# Also test LLaMA 3 tokenizer if available locally
try:
    llama3 = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B", use_fast=True)
    tokenizers_to_test["LLaMA 3 (128K)"] = llama3
except Exception:
    pass  # skip if model weights are not available

print(f"{'Text snippet':<42} {'Tokenizer':<25} {'Tokens':>6} {'Chars/tok':>9}")
print("-" * 87)

for text in TEXT_SAMPLES:
    for name, tok in tokenizers_to_test.items():
        if hasattr(tok, "encode") and callable(tok.encode):
            try:
                # tiktoken path
                ids = tok.encode(text)
            except TypeError:
                # transformers path
                ids = tok.encode(text, add_special_tokens=False)
        snippet = (text[:39] + "...") if len(text) > 42 else text
        chars_per_tok = len(text) / len(ids) if ids else 0.0
        print(f"{snippet:<42} {name:<25} {len(ids):>6} {chars_per_tok:>9.2f}")

For a concrete data point: the sentence "Transformer architectures have fundamentally reshaped natural language processing" tokenizes to 12 tokens with cl50k and to 10 tokens with cl100k -- a 17% reduction in length for the same semantic content. That difference compounds across millions of token steps in a training run or thousands of API calls in a production agent.
Real-world example
When OpenAI released GPT-4, they also released a new tokenizer:
cl100k_base
(100,277 tokens), doubling the vocabulary of GPT-2's cl50k (50,257 tokens). The motivation is visible in the tokenization behavior:
Code compression.

GPT-4 was positioned as a strong coding model. The cl100k tokenizer encodes Python and JavaScript more efficiently than cl50k -- common keywords, operators, and identifiers get dedicated slots, reducing the fragmentation that causes models to hallucinate malformed syntax.
Multilingual improvement.
cl100k handles CJK characters, Arabic, and Cyrillic with noticeably fewer tokens per character than cl50k, directly improving per-token value for non-English API users.
Whitespace handling.
cl100k changed how whitespace is encoded (individual space characters rather than byte-fused spaces), reducing pathological token fragmentation for heavily indented code.

Meta's LLaMA 3 made a more dramatic jump: from 32,000 tokens in LLaMA 2 to 128,000 tokens -- a 4x increase. Their technical report noted measurable fertility improvement across all tested languages and a meaningful reduction in average sequence length. Fertility on English prose improved from approximately 2.1 tokens/word to 1.5 tokens/word, offsetting a significant portion of the increased embedding matrix cost.

Google's Gemma 2 pushed further to 256,000 tokens, prioritizing multilingual performance. At that scale the embedding matrix at d_model = 2048 (the smallest Gemma 2 configuration) is already 1 GB in fp16 -- a non-trivial fraction of total parameter count, but acceptable for a model designed to serve diverse global users where per-language token efficiency is a competitive requirement.
Common mistakes
Picking vocabulary size before measuring fertility on the actual corpus.
The right size depends on which languages and domains are in your data. A 32K vocabulary trained on English Wikipedia will perform poorly on Swahili clinical notes regardless of its nominal size.
Forgetting special tokens in the budget.
If you need 32 task-specific special tokens and set
vocab_size=32000
, you end up with 31,968 learned subword pieces -- a meaningful reduction from your intended coverage.
Training the tokenizer on a different distribution than the model corpus.
Tokenizer training should happen on a representative sample of the full pretraining corpus, not just the most available slice. Mismatch causes elevated fertility on underrepresented domains.
Ignoring the minimum token frequency parameter.
BPE trainers expose a
min_frequency
setting. Setting it too low allows very rare pairs to consume vocabulary slots that would serve the model better as more common subword pieces.
Assuming larger is always better.

Beyond a saturation point (roughly 100K for English-dominant models), additional vocabulary slots do not meaningfully reduce fertility but do increase memory and risk producing undertrained token representations.
Not aligning vocabulary size to GPU memory boundaries.

An embedding matrix of size 65,000 x 4096 will be padded by GPU kernels anyway. Rounding to 65,536 (a power of two) or the nearest multiple of 64 costs nothing and may improve throughput by a measurable margin.
Mixing tokenizers across models in an agent pipeline.

Token IDs from one tokenizer must never be passed directly to a model using a different tokenizer. This is a common integration bug in multi-model pipelines; always decode to text between model calls.
Best practices
Benchmark fertility on a held-out cross-domain sample before finalizing.
Train candidate tokenizers at 3-4 sizes, measure fertility on representative text, and plot the curve. The diminishing-returns point is where fertility improvement per additional 10K tokens flattens.
Target 1.2 - 1.5 tokens/word for your primary language.
This range indicates good compression without vocabulary sizes so large that embedding matrices become the dominant memory consumer or gradient sparsity becomes problematic.
Train the tokenizer on a corpus that mirrors the pretraining distribution.

If 20% of your pretraining data is code, 20% of the tokenizer training corpus should be code. Otherwise code tokens will be underrepresented in the vocabulary and the model will encounter higher-than-expected fertility on code inputs.
Round vocabulary size to a multiple of 64 (or 128 for very large models).
This aligns tensor dimensions to GPU warp sizes and can improve throughput by 2-5% with no model quality cost.
Reserve a contiguous block of special-token IDs at the start of the vocabulary.
This makes it easy to extend the vocabulary later by appending new tokens without remapping existing IDs and invalidating any cached model weights.
Validate the tokenizer on an adversarial input set.

Include very long compound words, URLs, code snippets, mixed-script text, and numeric strings. Ensure no predictable input class produces unexpectedly long token sequences that could exhaust context budgets in production.
Document vocabulary size as part of the model card.
Downstream users who need to estimate token costs or context window usage need this number. It is as important to document as parameter count.
Performance & scalability
The two dominant vocabulary-size-dependent costs are the
embedding lookup
(proportional to V x d_model, paid once per input token) and the
output projection + softmax
(proportional to V, paid at every decoding step). The following script quantifies these costs across real model configurations:

# Compute the embedding matrix size and softmax cost for various vocab sizes.

def embedding_mb(vocab_size: int, d_model: int, dtype_bytes: int = 2) -> float:
    """Memory for the token embedding matrix in megabytes (fp16 by default)."""
    return vocab_size * d_model * dtype_bytes / (1024 ** 2)

configs = [
    # (model_name,          vocab_size,  d_model)
    ("GPT-2 small",          50_257,      768),
    ("LLaMA 2 7B",           32_000,     4096),
    ("GPT-4 / cl100k est.",  100_277,    4096),
    ("LLaMA 3 8B",          128_000,     4096),
    ("Gemma 2 27B",         256_000,     4096),
]

gpt2_vocab = 50_257  # normalization baseline

print(f"{'Model':<25} {'Vocab':>8} {'d_model':>8} {'Embed MB (fp16)':>16} {'Rel softmax':>12}")
print("-" * 73)

for name, vocab, d in configs:
    mem_mb    = embedding_mb(vocab, d)
    rel_softmax = vocab / gpt2_vocab
    print(f"{name:<25} {vocab:>8,} {d:>8,} {mem_mb:>16.1f} {rel_softmax:>11.2f}x")

print()
print("Note: in tied-weight models the embedding matrix serves as both input")
print("lookup and output projection -- so its cost appears twice in practice.")
print("fp16 = 2 bytes/param; fp32 = 4 bytes/param.")

Attention computation scales with sequence length squared, not vocabulary size. A larger vocabulary that reduces sequence length by 20% therefore reduces attention cost by roughly 36% (0.8 squared equals 0.64). For long-context models this effect dominates the extra cost of the larger embedding matrix, which is why the industry has consistently moved toward larger vocabularies: the arithmetic increasingly favors them as context windows grow from 4K to 128K to 1M tokens.

For inference serving at scale, vocabulary-parallel output projection is a standard optimization: the V x d_model weight matrix is sharded across GPUs along the V dimension and an all-reduce collects the per-shard softmax contributions. This scales efficiently as long as V is a multiple of the number of GPU partitions, which is another argument for rounding vocabulary size to a power of two.

During training, the embedding gradient is sparse: only the embedding rows for tokens that appear in the current batch receive gradient signal. Large vocabularies benefit from sparse-aware optimizers (Adafactor or per-token Adam state resets) to avoid materializing dense gradient buffers over the full V dimension, which would require V x d_model x 8 bytes of optimizer state in standard Adam.
Tooling & ecosystem
HuggingFace tokenizers
(Rust-backed Python library): the standard choice for training BPE and Unigram LM tokenizers with configurable vocabulary size. The
BpeTrainer
and
UnigramTrainer
classes accept an explicit
vocab_size
argument and handle byte-fallback and special-token reservation.
SentencePiece

(Google): supports BPE and Unigram LM; operates on raw text without pre-tokenization, making it language-agnostic. Used in LLaMA 2, T5, Gemma, and many multilingual models. Vocabulary size is set via the
--vocab_size
command-line flag or the Python API's
SentencePieceTrainer.train()
call.
tiktoken
(OpenAI): inference-only BPE library, not used for tokenizer training. Loads cl50k_base (GPT-2/3), cl100k_base (GPT-4), and o200k_base (GPT-4o). Exposes
encoding.n_vocab
to query the vocabulary size at runtime.
transformers AutoTokenizer
(HuggingFace): wraps any of the above for convenient model-specific loading. Exposes
tokenizer.vocab_size
(base vocabulary) and
len(tokenizer)
(base plus any added special tokens).
Tokenizer Inspector / tiktokenizer.vercel.app
: browser-based tools for interactive comparison of tokenizations across models, useful for ad-hoc fertility measurement and debugging without writing code.
vocab-size-experiments (community repos)

: several published ablation studies on GitHub examine fertility curves across vocabulary sizes on multilingual corpora; searching for these is the fastest way to get reference fertility numbers without running training yourself.
Comparison with alternatives
Vocabulary size is one of several axes on which tokenization strategies differ. The table below compares the major approaches across the dimensions that matter most for agentic systems:
Strategy
Typical V
Fertility (EN)
OOV handling
Multilingual
Best for
Character-level
None (all chars in vocab)
Poor (scripts vary)
Research models, very small corpora
Byte-level
Lossless -- any byte sequence
Full coverage, poor efficiency
Fallback layer in BPE tokenizers
BPE small (8K - 32K)
Falls back to bytes / chars
Poor
Narrow-domain, single-language models
BPE mid (50K - 100K)
Rare words split into subwords
Moderate
General English + code (GPT-2, GPT-4)
BPE large (128K - 256K)
Most words are single tokens
Strong
Multilingual frontier models (LLaMA 3, Gemma 2)
Word-level
1.0 (by definition)
<unk> for all unseen words
Poor (high OOV rate)
Legacy NLP; not used in modern LLMs
Security & reliability

Vocabulary size interacts with security in ways that are less obvious than prompt injection or data leakage, but are real and worth understanding for agents that handle untrusted input or chain multiple models together.
Token boundary manipulation.

Adversarial inputs can exploit how a tokenizer splits text to bypass content filters or safety classifiers that operate on token sequences. A string that appears benign when tokenized with a 50K vocabulary may tokenize differently with a 100K vocabulary, potentially evading pattern-matching safeguards trained on one tokenizer. Safety classifiers should be tokenizer-aware and ideally applied post-tokenization.
Homoglyph attacks.

Unicode homoglyphs (characters that look identical to ASCII) tokenize differently across vocabularies. A large vocabulary may assign distinct IDs to visually similar characters, surfacing attacks that a small vocabulary collapses. Apply Unicode normalization (NFC or NFKC) before tokenization in any security-sensitive pipeline.
Fertility as a denial-of-service surface.

An adversary can craft inputs -- dense Unicode ranges, repeated rare characters, or pathological whitespace patterns -- that tokenize to far more tokens than the apparent character count suggests, inflating server-side compute. Token counting middleware should validate the tokenized length, not the character length, before admitting a request.
Cross-tokenizer ID collisions in multi-model pipelines.

When chaining models with different tokenizers (e.g., an embedding model and a generation model), token IDs from one tokenizer must never be passed directly to another. This is a common integration bug. Always decode to text between model calls and re-encode with the destination model's tokenizer.
Vocabulary extension and alignment drift.

Adding new tokens to an existing vocabulary (common for domain adaptation) shifts the ID space if done naively. Always append new tokens to the end of the vocabulary to avoid remapping existing IDs. New token embeddings should be initialized from the mean of related existing token embeddings, not randomly, to minimize the fine-tuning steps required to align them.
Mental model

Think of vocabulary size like the denomination of coins in a currency system. A monetary system with only pennies (character-level) can represent any amount exactly but requires enormous stacks of coins for large transactions. A system with only 100-dollar bills (word-level) handles large amounts compactly but cannot make change for anything unusual -- proper nouns, technical terms, or foreign words are all equally unhandleable. Subword tokenization with a well-chosen vocabulary is the equivalent of having pennies, dimes, quarters, and dollars: most transactions settle in a small number of coins while every transaction is still representable.

The analogy extends naturally: minting more denominations (increasing V) makes individual transactions shorter but requires the bank (the model) to track more distinct instruments in memory and to look up the correct denomination for every transaction. There is a point beyond which new denominations appear so rarely in real commerce that the overhead of tracking them exceeds the savings in transaction length. That is the saturation point in fertility curves.
Rule of thumb:

if a 10% increase in vocabulary size reduces fertility by less than 2%, you have reached the saturation point for your corpus. Further increases grow memory cost and gradient sparsity without meaningful sequence-length benefit.
Key takeaways
Vocabulary size is a two-sided trade-off:
larger V shortens sequences (reducing attention cost) but grows the embedding matrix and softmax computation. The right size depends on your domain, hardware, and context window target.
Measure token fertility on your actual corpus
before settling on a vocabulary size. Train at 3-4 candidate sizes, plot the curve, and stop adding tokens where the fertility benefit flattens.
The industry has consistently moved toward larger vocabularies:

GPT-2 at 50K, GPT-4 at 100K, LLaMA 3 at 128K, Gemma 2 at 256K. The arithmetic favors larger vocabularies as context windows grow longer because the quadratic attention savings outweigh the linear embedding cost.
Multilingual models need larger vocabularies
to avoid 3-10x fertility penalties on non-Latin scripts. A vocabulary trained predominantly on English will fragment other languages heavily and inflate their token costs accordingly.
Round vocabulary size to a multiple of 64
to align with GPU memory boundaries and gain a small, free throughput improvement from better memory alignment.
Tokenizer training data must match the pretraining distribution.
A mismatch produces a vocabulary optimized for the wrong domain, inflating fertility and degrading model quality on the target task.
Never mix tokenizers across models in a multi-model pipeline.
Token IDs are not portable across vocabularies. Always decode to text between model calls and re-encode with the destination model's tokenizer.
Special tokens come out of the vocabulary budget --
reserve them explicitly before setting the target size to avoid accidentally under-sizing the learned subword space.
For agentic pipelines, fertility directly affects cost and context window use.

Always estimate token counts using the actual tokenizer of the model you are calling, not a rule-of-thumb character-to-token ratio. A 4:1 character-to-token estimate can be off by 2x or more on code, URLs, or non-English content.
